speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape 6. Today is January 2, 1997. I'm interviewing Shirley Warman. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I asked if you thought of the Holocaust often.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Especially when it comes a holiday. I miss my family. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My husband, and the family. But my children knows already.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happens on the holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm very sad. But I always with my children. I'm not alone in the house. Every holiday, I'm by my son, Karl. If not with them, I'm, I am, I am at my daughter-- my daughter-in-law's sisters. We all together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any dreams of the Holocaust?

speaker: interviewee
text: The beginning-- the beginning. I used to go and knock on the-- I saw-- I heard an airplane going. I knock my neighbor's door. I said let's go out. It's going to be-- right away, it's going to-- they're going to bombs. They said, don't worry. You in America. It's nothing there. And I-- you know, I'm not--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about now?

speaker: interviewee
text: It comes back all the time to me, you know. It does. I was in Washington for three days, in the Holocaust Museum. I was very sick, very sick, but I said I have to go see it. I saw my city. I find my city. I put the names of my family. But I couldn't see it. It's so high, you cannot see.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you were very sick. Can you describe that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Emotional. Yeah, I was-- you know, everything gets-- saw things in Majdanek and all over. It's very sickness. And we saw the interview, a family was talking like I am talking now to you. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you cry?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I cried a lot. Everybody did. And in Florida we would go, we always go see the monument. I was by the opening. With 110 degree, the heat was so high. And I was by the opening. And every year when I'm there, this is mine-- [NON-ENGLISH]. This is like you go every year, you go to the cemetery, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned during this interview that you always wanted to survive.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I, I, even now, when I'm very sick, I always think positive. I'm-- it's going to go through. Everybody knows already. Everybody knows. I always said, it's going to be OK. You see, I'm a survivor. And so far--

speaker: interviewer
text: What does it mean to be a survivor?

speaker: interviewee
text: It means you alive. I don't like to cry on somebody's shoulders. I cry a lot, but nobody sees me. I belong to two organizations, to the Circle. I'm on the committee there. They have meetings. They have dances. They had a beautiful party, a Hanukkah party. I'm involved. Now I'm the new [NON-ENGLISH] to the society. I'm treasury. And I have a lot of people around me. And they love me, and I love every one of them. They meet, they had [NON-ENGLISH], you know. Life is good. In a way, it's-- life is good. It could be worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your Holocaust experiences affect your belief in God and religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not just the Holocaust. when my husband died, got killed, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I said, I don't believe in anything. A young man, he was not even 52. He got a whole life ahead of him. If it could happen to him, nothing, nothing matters. But I do go the holidays. I go to the shul for [NON-ENGLISH]. That's all, nothing else. Nothing else.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else that you'd like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm happy. I have a wonderful family. They're all good to me. I couldn't anything better. [SOBS] I have a third generation already. I just pray I shouldn't, you know, get sick. And I shouldn't need their help. So far, I'm very-- everything is good. After-- today it's all right, tomorrow, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What message would you like to convey to your family and future generations?

speaker: interviewee
text: They shouldn't get-- they shouldn't go through this what I went through. They should be happy. They should stick together, means a lot. And then-- [SOBS] that's all there-- so far, it's very good. Very, very good. I have two daughter-in-laws. I don't see like two daughter-in-laws, like daughters.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are their names, your daughter-in-laws?

speaker: interviewee
text: One is Susan, she's in Florida. And one here is June. She's-- you cannot ask for anything-- better than my son. She comes. She takes me either I'm there. There's no place, what she couldn't take me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is somebody at the door? Can I go? Oh, no, no, no. Oh, I'm sorry. Oh, let it be. Well, thank you very much--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, thanks a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: --For sharing your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. This is my mother, my father. They were born in Poland, in Piastow. And this picture taking before the war, long, time ago. And I had an uncle, my mother's brother in Paris, it's after the war when he find me, so he sent me this picture.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the names of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Laia. And my father's name was Kuna. I'm starting from my left. My sister-- I can't see good. This is my older sister. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Her name is-- you know, I can't see good. I'm starting from my right. This is my younger sister. She is Ruchel. The other one is the, the second one, is Masha. And then comes my father is Kuna. And my mother is Laia. And my older brother is Joseph. And the youngest, the middle one is, is Rivka. Then comes Sura, that's me, and my brother Simcha.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was-- this was taken in Poland, maybe 1935 or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got this picture-- my mother had a brother in Paris. He had all the pictures. Then when they find each other, I was there in 1970-- 1970. I was three weeks there. He gave me all the pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your, your uncle's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My uncle's name was Shmiel. I'm starting from my right. This is my oldest brother, Joseph. And this is wife, Shaindel. This picture was taken in 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, in Lublin, [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH] 24.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you get this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got this same picture from mine uncle from Paris. This is my, my-- this is my brother, Simcha. I got to say this, this picture is taken in 1937. I got the same picture from mine uncle from Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Lublin [NON-ENGLISH] 24. This picture is me. This is was taken on the beach. And this was taken in 1932.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in a village, out of, out, out of the city, in the country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Out of which city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Out of Lublin. OK, this little picture is taken in, in Lublin. He must be-- this was-- he was a young boy. He, he, he must be about eight or nine years old. He got this from his aunt in Toronto, Canada and was [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is this? You didn't say who this was.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was, this was my, this was my husband. This is my husband. His, [SOBS] his name was Ben Warman. This was taken in 1947, here in the United States. This is my husband, Benjamin Warman. This picture was taking at my son Karl's bar mitzvah, here in this house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when or what year that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: 13 years, have 1957, 1970, must be. This is my family at my grandson's bar mitzvah. I'm starting from the top from the left. This is my, my son, Irving.

speaker: interviewer
text: And mine grandson-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] no, this is, this is his son-in-law, Brent. After is-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] why can't I see it? This is, this is my-- my second son, Karl, and the august Jonathan, my grandson.

speaker: interviewee
text: My great-granddaughter, Deborah, my grandson, Andy His wife, my granddaughter, Beth. My friend, Herschel. Then I'm starting from my right. This is me and my daughter-in-law, June. My granddaughter, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Danielle, my granddaughter, Jennifer. And my daughter-in-law, Susan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken here in Brooklyn, my grandson's bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long ago was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: A, a year ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, this is, this is Rachel, my great-granddaughter. She was born December-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] December what? November.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, November 14, the 16th.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year?

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: You'll have to say it again. Say it again.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my great-granddaughter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name is Rachel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rachel-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Jaramillo. And she was born in, in, in November 14, 1996.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
